---
title: "Spotify"
author: "Nikhita Purohit"
date: "2026-03-18"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: "On this page"
    toc-depth: 3
    smooth-scroll: true
editor: source
categories: [Linear Regression]
---

![](images/clipboard-286704031.png)

The data set looks at Spotify Song features

### **Objective:** To analyze and explore Spotify audio features to understand the characteristics and patterns present in different songs.

### **1. Setting up R Packages**

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(readr)
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(ggformula)
library(janitor)
library(naniar)
library(visdat)
```

### **2. Reading Data**

```{r}
song <- readr::read_csv("../../data/SpotifySongPolularityAPIExtract.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
```

### **3. Examining and Cleaning Data**

```{r}
dim(song)
str(song)
```

```{r}
head(song)
tail(song)
```

```{r}
names(song)
```

```{r}
song_modified <- song %>% tidyr::drop_na()
```

```{r}
song_modified <- song %>%
  dplyr::mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor)) %>% 
  relocate(where(is.factor))
glimpse(song_modified)
```

```{r}
song_modified %>%
  DT::datatable(
    style = "default",
    caption = htmltools::tags$caption(
      style = "caption-side: top; text-align: left; color: black; font-size: 100%;", "Spotify Dataset (Clean)"
    ),
    options = list(pageLength = 10, autoWidth = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  DT::formatStyle(
    columns = names(song_modified),
    fontFamily = "Arial",
    fontSize = "12px",
  )
```

### **4. Data Dictionary**

**Quantitative Data**

1.  Acousticness (dbl): A measure (0–1) of how likely the track is acoustic.
2.  Danceability (dbl): A measure (0–1) of how suitable the track is for dancing.
3.  Duration (int): The length of the song in milliseconds.
4.  Energy (dbl): The intensity and activity level of the song (0–1).
5.  Instrumentalness (dbl): The likelihood (0–1) that the track contains no vocals.
6.  Key (int): The musical key of the track, represented numerically (0–11).
7.  Liveness (dbl): The probability (0–1) that the track was performed live.
8.  Loudness (dbl): The overall loudness of the track in decibels (dB).
9.  Mode (int): Indicates whether the song is in a major (1) or minor (0) key.
10. Speechiness (dbl): The presence of spoken words in a track (0–1).
11. Tempo (dbl): The speed of the track in beats per minute (BPM).
12. Time Signature (int): The number of beats per bar in the track.
13. Valence (dbl): The musical positivity of the track (0–1), where higher values sound happier.
14. Popularity (int): A score from 0–100 indicating how popular the song is on Spotify.

**Qualitative Data**

1.  Artist Name (string): The name of the artist(s) who performed the track.
2.  Track Name (string): The title of the song.
3.  Track ID (string): A unique identifier assigned to each song by Spotify.

```{r}
summary(song)
```

### **5. Graphs**

```{r}
song_modified %>%
  gf_histogram(~ acousticness,
               fill = "cyan4",
               bins = 20) %>%
  gf_labs(title = "Accousticness",
          x = "Acousticness",
          y = "Number of Songs")

song_modified %>%
  gf_histogram(~ danceability,
               fill = "blue",
               bins = 20) %>%
  gf_labs(title = "Danceability",
          x = "Danceability",
          y = "Number of Songs")

# converting the duration from milliseconds to minutes because it is easier to understand

song_modified_minutes <- song_modified %>%
mutate(duration_min = round(duration_ms / 60000, 2))

song_modified_minutes %>%
  gf_histogram(~ duration_min,
               fill = "orange",
               bins = 20) %>%
  gf_labs(title = "Duration",
          x = "Duration (min)",
          y = "Number of Songs")

song_modified %>%
  gf_histogram(~ loudness,
               fill = "hotpink",
               bins = 20) %>%
  gf_labs(title = "Loudness",
          x = "Loudness",
          y = "Number of Songs")

song_modified %>%
  gf_histogram(~ tempo,
               fill = "violet",
               bins = 20) %>%
  gf_labs(title = "Tempo",
          x = "Tempo",
          y = "Number of Songs")

song_modified %>%
  gf_histogram(~ popularity,
               fill = "tan",
               bins = 20) %>%
  gf_labs(title = "Popularity",
          x = "Popularity",
          y = "Number of Songs")
```

```{r}
song_modified_minutes %>%
gf_point(popularity ~ tempo, data = song_modified_minutes, color = 'pink', size = 2) %>%
  gf_smooth(color = 'black') %>% 
  gf_labs(title = "Popularity vs Tempo",
          x = "Popularity",
          y = "Tempo")
```

```{r}
song_modified_minutes %>%
gf_point(popularity ~ duration_min, data = song_modified_minutes, color = 'lightgreen', size = 2) %>%
  gf_smooth(color = 'black') %>% 
  gf_labs(title = "Popularity vs Duration",
          x = "Popularity",
          y = "Duration (minutes)")
```

```{r}
song_modified_minutes %>%
gf_point(popularity ~ loudness, data = song_modified_minutes, color = 'darkturquoise', size = 2) %>%
  gf_smooth(color = 'black') %>% 
  gf_labs(title = "Popularity vs Loudness",
          x = "Popularity",
          y = "Loudness")
```

```{r}
song_modified_minutes %>%
gf_point(popularity ~ liveness, data = song_modified_minutes, color = 'slategrey', size = 2) %>%
  gf_smooth(color = 'black') %>% 
  gf_labs(title = "Popularity vs Liveness",
          x = "Popularity",
          y = "Liveness")
```

```{r}
popularity_vs_loudness_lm <- lm(popularity ~ loudness, data = song_modified_minutes)
popularity_vs_loudness_lm
song_modified_minutes %>%
  gf_point(popularity ~ loudness, color = 'maroon') %>%
  gf_lm(color = "black")%>%
  gf_labs(title = "Popularity vs Loudness",
          x = "Loudness",
          y = "Popularity")
```

```{r}
summary(popularity_vs_loudness_lm)
```

```{r}
popularity_vs_lid_lm <- lm(popularity ~ loudness + instrumentalness + danceability, 
                       data = song_modified_minutes)
popularity_vs_lid_lm
```

```{r}
summary(popularity_vs_lid_lm)
```

### **6. Predicting Model**

```{r}
test1 = data.frame(loudness = -10.08)

predict(popularity_vs_loudness_lm, newdata = test1)
```

```{r}
test2 = data.frame(loudness = -9.008,
                   instrumentalness = 8.47e-01,
                   danceability = 0.740)

predict(popularity_vs_lid_lm, newdata = test2)
```

### **7. Prediction using train and test data**

```{r}
rows <- nrow(song_modified_minutes)
rows
0.75 * rows
```

```{r}
train_rows <- sample(1:nrow(song_modified_minutes), 97998)
```

```{r}
train_data = song_modified_minutes[train_rows, ]
```

```{r}
test_data = song_modified_minutes[-train_rows, ]
```

1.  everything -\> popularity

```{r}
spotify_lm1 <- lm(popularity ~  acousticness + danceability + duration_min +                                        energy + instrumentalness + key + valence +
                                loudness + speechiness + tempo + time_signature +
                                valence,
                                data = train_data)

summary(spotify_lm1)
```

```{r}
predicted_values1 <- predict(spotify_lm1, newdata = test_data)
```

2.  only significant -\> popularity

```{r}
spotify_lm2 <- lm(popularity ~  acousticness + danceability +                                                       energy + instrumentalness + valence +
                                loudness + speechiness + time_signature +
                                valence,
                                data = train_data)

summary(spotify_lm2)
```

```{r}
predicted_values2 <- predict(spotify_lm2, newdata = test_data)
```

3.  energy and vibes model

```{r}
spotify_lm3 <- lm(popularity ~ danceability + valence + energy,
                  data = train_data)
summary(spotify_lm3)
```

```{r}
predicted_values3 <- predict(spotify_lm3, newdata = test_data)
```

4.  technical model

```{r}
spotify_lm4 = lm(popularity ~ danceability + valence + energy,
                 data = train_data)
summary(spotify_lm4)
```

```{r}
predicted_values4 <- predict(spotify_lm4, newdata = test_data)
```

5.  beats and rhythms model

```{r}
spotify_lm5 = lm(popularity ~ danceability + time_signature + tempo,
                 data = train_data)
summary(spotify_lm5)
```

```{r}
predicted_values5 <- predict(spotify_lm5, newdata = test_data)
```

6.  energy and loudness model

```{r}
spotify_lm6 = lm(popularity ~ danceability + energy + loudness + instrumentalness,
                 data = train_data)
summary(spotify_lm6)
```

```{r}
predicted_values6 <- predict(spotify_lm6, newdata = test_data)
```

7.  vibes model

```{r}
spotify_lm7 <- lm(popularity ~ liveness + acousticness + danceability + energy + valence,
                  data = train_data)
summary(spotify_lm7)
```

```{r}
predicted_values7 <- predict(spotify_lm7, newdata = test_data)
```

Finding RMSE for each linear model

```{r}
sqrt(mean((predicted_values1-test_data$popularity)^2))
sqrt(mean((predicted_values2-test_data$popularity)^2))
sqrt(mean((predicted_values3-test_data$popularity)^2))
sqrt(mean((predicted_values4-test_data$popularity)^2))
sqrt(mean((predicted_values5-test_data$popularity)^2))
sqrt(mean((predicted_values6-test_data$popularity)^2))
sqrt(mean((predicted_values7-test_data$popularity)^2))

```

Therefore, we can conclude that the first model \[ everything -\> popularity \] is the most successful linear model because it has the lowest RMSE.

### **8. Summary**

I found this dataset interesting because I never knew how many factors affect the success of a song. I converted duration from milliseconds to minutes. Made me question why they even recorded it in milliseconds because it is so hard to compare it that way. Tempo and popularity showed a very slight linear relation, so I discarded it. Popularity vs duration showed a slight inverse relation, showing that longer songs aren't as popular. Popularity vs loudness showed a significant linear relation so I built the first model using this. I also tried creating a model of \[popularity vs (loudness, instrumentalness and danceability)\]. The adjusted R\^2 values of both models are pretty low but the second model proves to be more accurate than the first.
